exploration_areas.title: Phase 5→6 Pipeline: Sliding Window Design
exploration_areas.focus: Is the min(3, remaining_slides) window size optimal? Could it be adaptive?

current_design.mechanism: content-core sends `draft_slide_ready(index=N)` per-slide. Lead launches slide-core immediately. Sliding window of min(3, remaining_slides) parallel slide-core agents.
current_design.content_core_behavior: agents/content-core.md:81 — 'After writing EACH slide draft, send draft_slide_ready(index=N) to lead (enables pipeline with Phase 6).'

resource_analysis.per_slide_agents: Each active slide requires 1 slide-core (opus) + 1 review-core (sonnet) + potential fix rounds. With window=3, peak concurrent agents: 3 slide-core + 3 review-core = 6 parallel agents.
resource_analysis.content_core_concurrent: 1 content-core (opus) still running drafts while slide-core agents process earlier slides.
resource_analysis.peak_total: At peak: 1 content-core (opus) + 3 slide-core (opus) + 3 review-core (sonnet) = 7 parallel agents (4 opus + 3 sonnet).
resource_analysis.cost_implication: Opus agents are expensive. 4 concurrent opus is aggressive.

findings.finding: Fixed window size ignores resource heterogeneity
findings.detail: slide-core uses opus (expensive, slower) while review-core uses sonnet (cheaper, faster). The window treats them as a unit but their latency profiles differ significantly. A slide-core agent takes much longer than review-core, creating review backlogs.

findings.finding: Window size does not account for fix loop overhead
findings.detail: When a slide enters a fix loop (score 5.0-6.9, max 2 rounds), it holds a slot in the window while going through design→review→fix→review cycles. Other slides queue behind it. A slide scoring 5.5 with 2 fix rounds effectively takes 3x the wall-clock time of a passing slide, blocking window throughput.

findings.finding: No backpressure mechanism from review to design
findings.detail: If all 3 window slots are in fix loops simultaneously, no new slides can start even if content-core has produced more drafts. There is no escalation or window expansion to prevent stalls.

findings.finding: Adaptive window could improve throughput for large decks
findings.detail: For a 15-slide deck, window=3 means max 5 batches serially. If early slides pass quickly (score>=7, no fix rounds), the window could expand to 4-5. Conversely, if fix loops are common, shrinking to 2 reduces wasted concurrent opus tokens. An adaptive policy: start at 3, expand by 1 if last 2 slides passed without fixes, shrink by 1 if last slide entered fix loop.

exploration_areas.title: Phase 6 Fix Loop: Scoring Calibration and Budget
exploration_areas.focus: Is the scoring model calibrated? Are 2 rounds sufficient?

current_design.pass_conditions: Weighted score >= 7.0 AND Layout >= 6 AND Readability >= 6 AND no Critical issues

fix_mechanism.structured_fixes: reviewer.md:106-138 — Fix suggestions are structured JSON with element, selector_hint, attribute, current, target, severity, reason.
fix_mechanism.slide_core_application: agents/slide-core.md:49 — 'Fixes are deterministic patches, not generative reinterpretation.'
fix_mechanism.regeneration_behavior: commands/ppt.md:189-198 — Score < 3.0 regenerates without fixes_json, producing fresh layout from draft reference.

findings.finding: Score 5.0-6.9 band may be too wide for uniform treatment
findings.detail: A slide scoring 5.0 needs +2.0 points to pass; a slide scoring 6.8 needs +0.2. Both get the same 2-round budget. The 5.0 slide is unlikely to pass in 2 rounds if each round typically improves score by 0.5-1.0 points. Consider splitting: 5.0-5.9 gets 2 rounds but lower expectations (accept at 6.5?), 6.0-6.9 gets 2 rounds targeting 7.0+.

findings.finding: Hard gates can cause pass/fail cliff at score thresholds
findings.detail: A slide with weighted score 7.2 but Layout=5 fails the hard gate. The fix loop targets Layout improvement, but if slide-core patches Layout from 5→6, other scores might shift. No evidence the fix loop verifies that non-targeted criteria don't regress.

findings.finding: Deterministic patching may not improve Layout or Readability scores
findings.detail: The fix mechanism (slide-core.md:49) treats fixes as 'deterministic patches, not generative reinterpretation.' But Layout Balance and Readability issues often require structural changes (moving cards, reflowing text), not just attribute tweaks. A font-size change is a patch; a layout reflow is generative. The fix JSON format (element/attribute/current/target) biases toward attribute patches.

findings.finding: No score trajectory tracking across fix rounds
findings.detail: slide-status.json records final score and fix_rounds count, but not per-round scores. Without trajectory data, it is impossible to know if fixes are improving scores or if they plateau. This makes it hard to tune the budget or detect fix-loop thrashing (score oscillating around threshold).

findings.finding: Regeneration (score < 3.0) still only gets 1 attempt
findings.detail: commands/ppt.md:196-198 — After regeneration, re-run review-core. If still failing, accept with quality note. But regeneration produces a completely new layout, which might also score < 3.0. There is no retry count for regeneration itself, meaning a fundamentally difficult slide (e.g. dense dashboard type) could be stuck at low quality.

exploration_areas.title: Phase 3 Material Collection: Parallel Search and Serial Merge
exploration_areas.focus: Could the serial merge be smarter?

current_design.parallel_agents: One research-core (sonnet) per major section topic. Each writes to isolated file (materials-${SLUG}.md).
current_design.merge_strategy: Lead serially merges all per-topic files into materials.md after all agents complete.
current_design.incremental_search: agents/research-core.md:55 — collect mode reads research-context.md to skip already-covered content.

findings.finding: Serial merge is a bottleneck only in theory, not practice
findings.detail: The merge happens after all agents complete. For a typical 3-5 section deck, this is 3-5 files of a few KB each. The lead can concatenate + deduplicate in seconds. The real bottleneck is waiting for the slowest research-core agent, not the merge itself.

findings.finding: No cross-section deduplication during merge
findings.detail: Two research-core agents searching 'AI industry trends' and 'AI enterprise adoption' may find overlapping content. The serial merge is described as concatenation — no dedup logic is specified. materials.md could contain duplicate data points, wasting tokens for content-core in Phase 4.

findings.finding: Section extraction from requirements.md is implicit
findings.detail: commands/ppt.md:113 says 'Extract section topics from requirements.md' but no structured format for sections is defined. requirements.md is free-form (written from user answers + research context per line 109). The lead must infer section boundaries, which is fragile for non-obvious topic structures.

findings.finding: No quality gate between Phase 3 and Phase 4
findings.detail: The fallback rule (line 337) says 'if material collection partially fails, continue with available materials.' But there is no minimum threshold — if 4/5 agents fail, content-core still runs. Outline quality would suffer silently.

findings.finding: Smart merge could rank and prioritize materials
findings.detail: Instead of simple concatenation, the merge could: (1) deduplicate data points appearing in multiple section files, (2) rank materials by source authority and recency, (3) annotate which sections each material supports. This would make materials.md a more useful input for content-core.

exploration_areas.title: Resume Detection: Edge Cases in --run-id Logic
exploration_areas.focus: Are there edge cases in artifact-presence-based resume?

current_design.logic: Sequential artifact check: slide-status.json → draft-manifest.json → outline.json → materials.md → requirements.md → research-context.md → Nothing
current_design.slide_status_resume: commands/ppt.md:167 — On resume: read slide-status.json, skip completed slides, continue from first incomplete.

findings.finding: Partial write to slide-status.json causes data loss
findings.detail: slide-status.json is updated after each slide completes (commands/ppt.md:158-166). If the process crashes mid-write, the JSON could be truncated/invalid. On resume, JSON parse fails, and the entire Phase 6 might restart from scratch, redoing completed slides. No atomic write or backup strategy is defined.

findings.finding: outline.json present but outdated (user modified requirements)
findings.detail: If a user resumes at Phase 5 (outline.json exists, no draft-manifest.json), but they manually edited requirements.md between runs, the outline is stale. The resume logic trusts artifact presence but does not validate content consistency between artifacts.

findings.finding: draft-manifest.json exists but drafts/ is incomplete
findings.detail: content-core writes draft-manifest.json after all drafts are written (agents/content-core.md:80-81). But if the process crashes between writing the last draft SVG and writing draft-manifest.json, resume incorrectly skips to Phase 6 start. The drafts may actually be complete (SVGs all written) but the manifest is missing — this is the wrong resume point.

findings.finding: No resume for Phase 4 outline revision loop
findings.detail: If the user was in the outline revision loop (Phase 4, step 2-3) and the process crashed, resume detects 'outline.json exists but no draft-manifest.json → resume at Phase 5'. This skips the user's pending approval/revision, potentially using an unapproved outline.

findings.finding: Phase 2 resume skips re-research
findings.detail: If research-context.md exists but requirements.md does not, resume goes to Phase 2 user confirmation. This is correct. But if both exist (requirements.md present, materials.md absent → resume Phase 3), there is no way to re-trigger Phase 2 research if the original research was stale or insufficient.

exploration_areas.title: Holistic Review: Could Partial Checks Catch Issues Earlier?
exploration_areas.focus: Running holistic review after ALL individual slides vs. incremental

current_design.timing: After all individual slides pass, run review-core mode=holistic. Evaluates cross-slide consistency, visual rhythm, narrative arc, pacing.
current_design.reviewer_spec: reviewer.md:180-191 — Holistic checks: visual rhythm, color story, narrative arc, style consistency, pacing.
current_design.gate: commands/ppt.md:206 — If holistic score < 7, flag issues but do not block delivery.

findings.finding: Late holistic review cannot fix systemic issues cost-effectively
findings.detail: If the holistic review finds 'accent color overused on every slide' or 'monotonous layout pattern', fixing requires re-running slide-core for multiple slides. By Phase 6.4, all slide-core+review-core work is done. The holistic review is advisory (does not block delivery), so systemic issues are just flagged, not fixed.

findings.finding: Partial holistic check after every 3-4 slides is feasible
findings.detail: After the window produces 3-4 completed slides, a lightweight cross-slide consistency check could run: (1) are layouts varying enough? (2) is accent color usage escalating? (3) are shadow/border-radius consistent? This catches systemic issues early enough to adjust remaining slides.

findings.finding: Style consistency issues are cheapest to fix during generation
findings.detail: reviewer.md:187 checks 'Are shadows, border-radius, font sizes consistent across all slides?' These are style token compliance issues that slide-core should catch during generation, not in a post-hoc holistic review. If slide-core strictly reads style YAML (which it does per slide-core.md:40-41), consistency should be guaranteed by construction.

findings.finding: Narrative arc validation requires full deck — cannot be partial
findings.detail: The 'narrative arc' check (setup→tension→resolution) needs the complete slide set. This genuinely must wait until all slides are done. However, pacing checks (are there breathing slides between dense ones?) could run on sliding windows of 3-5 consecutive slides.

findings.finding: Color story escalation is a generative concern, not a review concern
findings.detail: reviewer.md:185 — 'Does accent color usage escalate toward key slides?' This is something the outline or draft phase should plan for, not something the holistic review should discover post-hoc. outline-architect.md does not mention accent escalation planning. Adding a 'color weight' hint to outline.json pages would enable slide-core to manage this proactively.

exploration_areas.title: Phase 2→3 Transition: Requirements to Material Collection
exploration_areas.focus: Is section extraction from requirements.md reliable?

current_design.mechanism: Lead extracts section topics from requirements.md, then launches parallel research-core agents per section.
current_design.requirements_source: commands/ppt.md:109 — 'Write requirements.md from user answers combined with research context.'
current_design.user_input_structure: commands/ppt.md:101-106 — AskUserQuestion asks: target audience, purpose, key messages (3-5 points), tone, specific requirements.

findings.finding: requirements.md has no defined schema for section extraction
findings.detail: The lead must parse free-form prose in requirements.md to identify section topics. There is no markdown template, required headings, or structured format specified. If the lead writes requirements.md with sections like '## Target Audience', '## Key Messages', '## Content Requirements', the section topics must be inferred from content, not structure.

findings.finding: Key messages from user directly map to sections
findings.detail: The Phase 2 AskUserQuestion asks for 'Key messages (3-5 core points to convey)'. These 3-5 points naturally become the section topics for Phase 3 collection. The transition would be reliable if requirements.md explicitly lists these as a bullet list under a standard heading. Currently this is implicit.

findings.finding: Section count determines Phase 3 parallelism cost
findings.detail: Each section launches a research-core (sonnet) agent. If requirements.md yields 5 sections, that is 5 parallel sonnet agents. For a simple 8-slide deck this is excessive. No cost/benefit check: 'Is this section topic specific enough to warrant a dedicated search agent?'

findings.finding: No feedback loop if collection reveals requirements gap
findings.detail: If research-core agents discover that a section topic has no available material (e.g. proprietary data, very niche topic), there is no mechanism to escalate back to Phase 2 for user clarification. The agent just writes what it found (possibly nothing useful). The gap only surfaces in Phase 4 when content-core tries to build an outline with thin material.

cross_cutting_findings.title: Agent cost model is implicit
cross_cutting_findings.detail: The pipeline spawns up to 7 concurrent agents (4 opus + 3 sonnet) during Phase 5-6 overlap. There is no cost awareness — no budget tracking, no throttling based on token spend, no preference for cheaper models for lower-risk work. slide-core validation checks (xmllint, font-size grep) run inside an opus agent, but could be a simple bash script.

cross_cutting_findings.title: No end-to-end latency model
cross_cutting_findings.detail: The pipeline has no estimates for how long each phase takes, making it impossible to optimize the critical path. Phase 6 is clearly the bottleneck (N slides × design + review + potential fix loops), but without latency measurements, the sliding window size is a guess.

cross_cutting_findings.title: Artifact consistency is trust-based, not validated
cross_cutting_findings.detail: Each phase reads artifacts from previous phases (e.g. content-core reads requirements.md and materials.md). But there is no schema validation, no checksums, no version tracking. A corrupted or manually edited artifact can cascade failures silently.

cross_cutting_findings.title: Communication protocol lacks structured message types
cross_cutting_findings.detail: Agents send messages like 'draft_slide_ready(index=N)', 'outline_ready', 'review_passed', 'review_failed'. These are informal string conventions, not typed message schemas. There is no guarantee that the lead correctly parses all message variants, especially error messages.

cross_cutting_findings.title: Pre-review validation duplicated between slide-core and review-core
cross_cutting_findings.detail: slide-core.md:53-75 runs xmllint, viewBox check, font-size minimum, safe area boundary after writing SVG. review-core.md:34-42 runs identical checks before LLM review. This is defensive but wasteful — if slide-core passes all checks, review-core's pre-checks should always pass. The duplication exists because slide-core might not have run checks (no enforcement), but the design intent is that it always does.

priority_ranked_issues.title: Deterministic patching cannot fix structural Layout/Readability issues
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Fix loop may be ineffective for the two highest-weighted criteria (Layout 25%, Readability 25%), causing slides to exhaust fix budget without improving

priority_ranked_issues.title: Partial write to slide-status.json can corrupt resume state
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Process crash during JSON write causes all completed slides to be lost on resume, wasting significant compute

priority_ranked_issues.title: Resume skips outline approval gate if outline.json exists
priority_ranked_issues.impact: User may get an unapproved outline used for production slides, violating the Hard Stop contract

priority_ranked_issues.title: Late holistic review cannot cost-effectively fix systemic issues
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Cross-slide consistency problems flagged but never fixed, delivered to user as warnings

priority_ranked_issues.title: Fix loops block window slots, reducing pipeline throughput
priority_ranked_issues.impact: A single slow-to-fix slide can block 33% of the pipeline window capacity

priority_ranked_issues.title: Score 5.0-6.9 band too wide for uniform 2-round budget
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Slides at 5.0 unlikely to reach 7.0 in 2 rounds; budget wasted

priority_ranked_issues.title: No cross-section deduplication in material merge
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Duplicate content wastes tokens in Phase 4 outline generation

priority_ranked_issues.title: No defined schema for section extraction from requirements.md
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Section topic identification is fragile and model-dependent

priority_ranked_issues.title: Color story escalation planned at wrong phase
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Post-hoc discovery of accent overuse when it should be planned in outline

priority_ranked_issues.title: No cost awareness in agent spawning
priority_ranked_issues.impact: Peak 4 opus + 3 sonnet concurrent agents with no budget control
